sentences.text: So I found this cousin, and I talked to her.
Everything is fine.
I say I would take you with your nephew if you have money, because without money it's no use.
It's not good for you.
It's not good for me.
We don't know when the war is going to be finished.
So she said I've got 12 20 gold dollar bills, I got.
This is what she got.
I say, all right, you got it.
So I say where's your nephew-- where's your husband's nephew?
He say he is out.
I'm going to let my friends find him and bring him here.
And they found them, and they brought them here.
Oh, yeah.
So she tells me a story.
I tell you, does anybody know where you have the money?
She say no, only I'm sorry I had to tell my nephew.
I have to tell him, because he bothered me.
They told him if we cannot draw no way together, maybe I'll be alive or you'll be alive, I would like to know where you have the money, Tante.
He called her Tante-- aunt.
I have to know where you have the money.
She didn't want to tell him, but later she figures maybe he's right.
So she had under the window in the ground, digged in, the money.
The 12 20 gold dollar bills.
So I say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] does-- so you sure [?
Belcher ?] was his name.
Belcher ?] Schtramer.
So and then he walks in.
Somebody brought him.
He walks in, and I said [?
Belcher, ?] you know you want to go.
I want to take you and your aunt.
When want to take you to our gentleman, to our nice man, a farmer.
And we hope your survive there, but I want-- I want to see-- I want to see the money.
So you're on your-- your aunt will show us where the money, and she say no, no.
It's no use.
It's no use to look over there.
I took the money out.
My aunt doesn't even know.
A friend of his, a watchmaker, lived next door, right door by door, was there.
And he gave him an idea.
You know, [?
Belcher-- ?] when the money-- everybody knows that your aunt has money.
So the money is not worth, and the money will be buried there.
You have to keep the money with you always.
And we have to run or we have to go someplace.
You have to keep your money with you.
He say how? what do you mean?
Yeah, you can take the money out.
And you put it in shoe, put a double sole.
You put a double sole, and it's closed.
And that's the way, when you have to run, you run, you have the money.
Otherwise, to go and dig, that's-- you have no time.
So you figure maybe he is right.
So he dig out the money.
And he say, but you know what?
You cannot put in all the money in your shoes.
Three on one side and three 20 gold bills on the other side, and I'll take the other six in my shoes.
He had his own, a lot of money.
He had a place even by a Polish woman already organized that she is going to-- she is going to keep it.
Now I say, [?
Belcher, ?] what are you talking about?
You gave him the six 20 [INAUDIBLE].
He gave you the-- that's not right to me.
I said you take off the shoes.
You don't have no more money here.
I bet you he gave you Polish zloties to put in here.
That's what happened.
He takes out the shoes, pick out the sole, there's six silver Polish zloties.
He took out the other six from him, and he stacked them away.
I say, here you don't have nowhere to go with them.
So then there-- then my-- then my cousin starts screaming and screaming, what do you do?
Why you listen to such a crook?
Why you listen?
Anyway, what's happened?
I called back to find my friend-- to find my friend.
I say here, listen.
You have a [INAUDIBLE].
You can-- you maybe know-- maybe somebody knows where Schmuckler is.
Schmuckler was no more there.
He was hiding already by a Gentile person, by a lady.
She made an extra bunker from him in her house.
She had a big house.
And he must have promised or gave her a lot of his own money.
So I say-- he say, I know where Schmuckler is hiding.
I say, can you take somebody else with you and go?
I couldn't go there.
But you go with another person.
Go to there, tell the lady-- tell the Polish lady if she is not going to chase him out from there, you're going to call the Gestapo, and we're going to tell them not you and not me.
There's no such thing, couldn't happen.
That's the way it happened.
And then they told the Polish lady what he did, she say what a terrible person he is.
He shouldn't be alive even.
He should-- he should have been killed, that he can do something like that.
He has enough his own money.
He was a watchmaker.
He had a lot of gold and diamonds even.
So anyway, she chased him out.
She gave him hell, and they brought him to that little ghetto, to the W ghetto.
They brought him.
And there was a Judenrat.
There was a Jewish committee.
He called them, and he said you have to give back.
So we got back only six.
He still gave him three and gave him three and the committee on the, you know.
I may not be smart, but I gave her six.
You know, so I figure maybe it'll be enough, whatever it is.
And I got a lot of friends, Ukrainian and Polish friends, somehow I'll get food.
But the meanwhile, we'll need some of them.
That's what we picked up.
There was a lot of material stacked.
Clothes, all kinds of beautiful clothes, suits for everything.
You know, the Jewish storekeeper, they had a lot of leather was there.
They had everything, they had.
And they smuggled that from one-- from one ghetto, from the big ghetto to that ghetto where they were.
Anyway, we went.
We went and I came to that Stefan Marko back, and we were sitting there, and we were hiding there, like for two and half, close to three months.
And then he has to go out in the field.
He has to go out and do work, you know.
And he had a guy, a young boy, like 12 or 13 years old, he came to take out his cows, to feed the cows all there.
He was afraid that he is going to catch us.
That little boy was a little devil.
And also a big anti-Semite the way he was talking.
So he said I'm afraid.
Leon, you'll have to go.
You have to go in the woods.
So I said all right, you're right.
I don't want to put you in trouble.
I don't want to put us in trouble.
And I went to a little forest, very small forest, not far from our Polish friend.
I used to go with him to school, with him and his sister.
They were our family.
Their name was Stefan.
Jasek Martko was their name.
And their son's name was also Jasek Martko.
That's the way they give names.
And I went in and I sit in the back of his big farm.
He had a big farm.
And I sit in his wheat, corn and wheat.
He was Polish.
He was Polish, yeah.
Christian.
And I was going-- yeah, Christian.
And I was going to the school, to the Polish school, four years or four and a half years, to this school.
And he was my close friend, very close friend in school.
Of that forest?
It's just a little forest, just like-- I don't even remember the name.
It was a very little forest.
Near Lubiana, another city.
Lubiana, by the village.
Not far from the village, also by the city of Jawarow [INAUDIBLE].
So and I went in that little forest.
I went and started building a bunker, and once I build the bunker, at one night I was all ready to finish up the bunker inside, my wife calls me up.
Leon, they caught us already.
Two boys, tall boys came in, they just heard something, you know, I was digging in the inside.

And I always had a little hacksaw here with me, always carrying it, then while I was building, I got all the materials for building, because Stefan Marko, I told him to give me all the materials for building a bunker.
So and I jumped out.
She said, Leon, they caught us already.
And I jumped out, and I came out, and I figured all right, maybe you know.
And this guy say, Leon [NON-ENGLISH]-- Leon what are you doing here?
And he grabbed me.
He came around, and he grabbed me.
And I say who is this?
This is my younger brother.
He was very small those-- you know.
I say what are you doing here?
What are you doing?
You're building here a bunker.
Such a bad spot.
Kids are common.
It's so close from the village.
They come in here for mushrooms to look, and what are you doing here?
I say I don't have nowhere to go for a while.
I figure for a while I say-- he say it's very bad for here.
So he ran home.
He brought milk and bread and butter and food and feed us all.
And he say but I'm very afraid.
I say I'm sitting even on your-- on your farm, in the back of your farm, I sitting there already for a couple days.
I'm sitting there.
I thought I'll have my time to catch you and talk to you what I plan to do, to go in the bigger forest.
So he say it's no good, but anyway all right.
And I covered everything up, before I finished, with trees and with everything.
Somehow, somehow, somehow the kids came for mushrooms, and they just find the place.
They found the place so they know [INAUDIBLE] they're Jews must be here in this bunker.
So they run to the village and tell everybody.
The whole village was so excited, they came out.
They're going to catch Jews in that bunker.
And they came out with hacksaws, with everything.
They don't know who.
Maybe they have guns, they were figuring.
Now, when this all happened, I had a dream.
I had a dream that I see all my-- all my friends and not friends, all young people come and they want to catch me.
They going around, and they're going closer and closer and closer.
And when I wake up, before I wake up, I had a dream that my mother came.
And my mother grabbed me all around.
She grabbed me all around.
She say [? mein kind, ?] nobody will do anything bad to you.
You'll-- you'll be alive.
You'll be alive, and nobody will touch you.
And when I woke up, I was so shaken.
And then my friend comes out, and he tells me-- gives me whistle and he tells me, Leon, don't go to the bunker.
There is people-- so many people there watching.
Before you're going to go to that bunker, they caught everything.
And they throw even the food what I gave you, whatever you have leftover, they throw on my farm.
Don't go there.
So OK, that's it.
So then I start figuring where to go and what to go.
Good thing what I never left any clothes, anything, but I had the clothes left by Stefan Marko.
And I gave him all the money.
I gave-- I just came.
I gave him all the money.
I say we'll need food or whatever we'll need.
Here's a lot of clothes and a lot of material.
I'm going to leave everything here by you.
Whatever I'll need, I'll come to you.
I know you.
You are-- you are-- you are-- you are not going to do anything to me with my money.
And I left the clothes there.
When I would have took it with me and the money, maybe they would catch everything.
So anyway, that's what happened.
And I told my friend what I want to go.
I want to go in the-- to [INAUDIBLE].
While we're there, I want to establish myself.
I'm going to build our big bunker.
I'm going to build, and whatever you can, you can bring me to the bunker.
I'll tell you where I am, because everybody has forest in the same place.
I said there's a big tree.
I still remember it in my head after so many years that there was a big tree, and the tree was going-- a white tree-- and the tree was going bended very, very much.
So he say I know where the tree.
There you're going to throw me in whatever food or whatever you have.
And I used to come back, and I said now I'll come back once in a while to you for food.
Everything was fine.
Everything was good.
And then after a couple days-- before we go-- before we go, I say we'll go from here, because I'm afraid already to be in this area.
I'll go in another section from the village.
In another area, there's always also a little forest, and I can stay there and hide there.
So I went to the other forest.
We took everything with us, and we were there in the forest.
And then it start pouring and raining.
It was unbelievable.
Before I came there, it start to pour.
It was like coming with [INAUDIBLE].
Unbelievable.
You are so soaking wet.
I say what the heck am I going to go through here in this rain and this everything?
Let's go to Stefan Marko back.
I have no choice.
I'll go to Stefan Marko, and I'll give him another order.
Maybe I'll go from him later, go to the big forest.
I'll go there.
And we were all soaking wet.
And they say where-- we schlepped.
And where you schlepping us?
Where are we going?
Let's get-- let them caught us already and forget about the whole thing.
And it was so dark, I didn't even see how to go to him.
So anyway, I figured out go this way and this way, and I'll come to the right place.
Now, when I come there, and I came there, I knock on his-- no, I didn't even knock on his door.
The letter was still there standing to go up.
And I went up.
Everybody pushed up on that ladder and came and sit there early in the morning.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] His wife comes in, Marinya-- Marinya was her name-- and I go closer and closer.
There was an opening, and she came to milk the cows, and I say good morning, Marinya.
Good morning.
In the Ukrainian [NON-ENGLISH] what are you doing here?
What's happened?
I saw everything what's going on.
They were running and, you know, they going to catch Jews, and they're going to kill him.
You know, I know what's happened.
How and what?
So I told her the story.
She say, but what do you want from our life?
Why should we be involved, it's so bad?
We want to stay alive too.
Why don't you go someplace else?
I say by the first day, I know where I'm going.
I'm not going to stay here, Marinya.
I want the best for you and for Stefan.
And they had a daughter and the grandma was there.
I say I don't want you to be doing that you're going to be harmed.
I want you to-- I wanted to tell you what I need.
You have to go to the city.
You have to buy me everything, whatever I need.
I need equipment.
I need a lot of-- and I talk to her husband, to Stefan, I need to make me a-- a special-- a special thing, a board with a handle, because I cannot do this in the wood, that I can make a bunker.
And I figured out how the bunker will look.
Just when I go in the bunker, I'll have a little tree, and this will close in.
I'll have a stepladder going into the bunker.
And I need gasoline.
I need candles, and I need parts, and I need pans to cook, and whatever everything.
I forgot-- only one thing I forgot is medication.
I even had bandages but no medication, no nothing.
Little bandages.
I even figured they were going to get burned or whatever.
So she went to the city in the daytime, because he was a little sick.
He had problem with the heart.
I think scared with us.
So she went to the city.
She brought me everything-- needles for sewing, cotton, complete everything, whatever, scissors-- to build a bunker, all kind of equipment-- a saw and knives, everything.
So she went, and she brought me this.
And the next day-- oh, yeah, and the main thing I told her, you know what, Marinya?
She say I'll give you everything.
I'll even give you the money.
I'll give you the material.
I said that's what I need, because I don't know.
After the war, don't forget it.
Don't forget.
You're going to be treated like a family, but for now she brought everything packed up-- material, everything, the money, because I trust her.
And she gave me everything back.
And the next night, 11:00, 12:00, we packed up and was started wandering.
It was very far, miles and miles away.
And we had to go through a bridge, through a lake.
And we came in-- we came in there.
Early in the morning we came in.
We were so tired, and I was wondering.
There was no heavy forest, and my-- and my, where we had, was very loose, like here a big tree, here big trees for [INAUDIBLE].
But I was looking for thick forest, just bushes and branches, a lot.
And we sat down, and we had to eat.
She gave us bread, and she gave us-- and then my Polish friend, his mother baked bread.
We had a lot of food with us.
So-- and we came, and we sat down early in the morning, and it was raining.
So I made from a material just to put on top of us so we wouldn't get so wet.
And we were eating.
In the meanwhile, people were already out for mushrooms, and there's raining.
And before it stops raining, they come from mushroom.
The mushrooms come out.
So they came in.
Two guys came in from the side and came in.
They saw us right away.
Oh, Jewish-- Jewish, like a Jewish [NON-ENGLISH].
A Jewish military or whatever they say.
Jews.
They saw we had a lot of equipment, a lot of-- they were afraid as we have guns.
And I have a gun also.
So they were afraid.
So one guy say, eh, let him go.
Let him go.
Let him go.
Don't touch him.
Let him go.
I know.
So anyway, they say let him go, and they went away.
Soon they went away.
Packed up everything, and we run.
Keep running and running till I find a big ditch in the woods-- a big ditch-- also took some trees, bushes, you know, packed it inside.
We hide inside, because I know they're going to look for other ones.
They'll take a big group, and then they're going to go and catch us and kill us.
So with me was my wife, Ruthie, Morris, and my father's cousin and her nephew-- and her nephew.
We were five people.
Oh, the nephew.
Belcher-- ?] Bernard.
Bernard Schtramer what his name is.
I Just remember.
You know, you keep forgetting.
And my cousins name was Rauch-- Rauch.
Blima Rauch was my cousin's name-- my father's cousin.
So and we ran and we hide there.
Soon we hide there.
Right away we hear screaming.
Look for the Jews.
They must be here.
We saw them.
And let's get them, and let's-- Ukrainian, everything in Ukrainian.
And anyway, they looked around and they didn't find us.
After they didn't find us, we sit there a whole day till the night.
When it got dark, I keep walking till I found a place where we can hide, where we can build a bunker.
First I found a place so we pack our packages, you know.
Covered everything up, and I start going to digging a hole.

Now, I start going digging a hole but so many branches with so many trees, I had to take every tree all around, with the tree and with the mud, take and put them away 20-- I'd say 20 yard, make clear the whole place.
Later dig out the mud.
The bunker was like 30-- 30 by 20 feet long.
Then start going, and I dig all of this out, all the mud.
I went and start going in different sections far away, cutting trees, round trees like this, maybe a little heavier, cut them, schlep them, bringing over here.
And you start building like this, throwing around, throw this way and one wall, the other wall, all around build up those trees, and the bunker all around.
Later I made for my wife a place where to cook.
So we had barley, we have all kind of other food, you know.
You cooking soup in a big pot.
So I cut two big tree-- you know, two trees.
They were so heavy, so heavy, putting over here.
Dig a hole and to make a fire with wood, make a fire, and put the pot on top.
And then she was cooking with water.
Brought water from a lake far away.
Went for water, brought water.
And she was cooking here.
And my wife and my cousin was sitting here.
I say watch.
If it start burning down already, those pieces of wood, you got two other pieces, and I got two more on the both sides to push it in.
But it wouldn't burn through.
It fell.
So I gave them the order, and I went there.
Then I hear a scream, screaming terrible.
I recognize the scream, a voice.
I say, you know, [?
Belcher, ?] let's drop everything and run.
And we ran.
I see something.
Oh, a wild animal caught or whatever, some-- I didn't-- came right away to my mind that will, and she let the wood burn-- burned and then not to change.
And I came, and I saw the scream and throwing yourselves.
He say hang me, kill me, do anything.
I don't want to live no more.
And then I start don't scream, don't cry.
Everybody start blowing.
I have no medication.
I don't have nothing, you know.
Put a little cold water.
It didn't help.
She was screaming.
Won't you put something.
She was blowing and blowing.
It went out like this.
Later I say, you know what?
I'm not going to stay here.
I'm going to run to the village, to Stefan Marko, and let him go and bring me medication from the city, from the drugstore, and then I'll come back.
But when I went at night, when I went there, I couldn't come back the same day.
In the day I couldn't walk.
She had to go to the city with a horse and wagon and go to the drugstore and bring me all the medications for burns.
And then at night, I'm going to walk and come back.
Now, she brought everything, whatever I needed for the medication, even extra I told.
And when I come back-- when I come back in the morning, my Ruthie is walking around.
I say what's happened?
So she picked up her dress, and she showed me-- all healed.
Everything healed, the whole thing, all the blisters from here, all the way up.
All the blisters.
So I say how did that happen?
What do you-- what did you do?
What kind-- you have no medication.
She say I'll tell you what's happened.
With all my pain and everything, and they were blowing, and I fall asleep.
And when I was sleeping, my mother came to me.
My mother came to me.
She grabbed me around.
And she say [GERMAN], you will survive only here.
Don't go from here no place.
Here you'll survive.
And then I woke up.
That's what happened.
Everything disappeared.
No blisters, no nothing, dry completely.
You don't have to put any medication.
I don't have to put on no medication at all.
So yeah, I say there is miracle.
People used to say there is miracles, but now I see there is something.
And that's the way we survived.
Only here in the woods, you survive.
Don't move from here no place, you'll survive.
Then, when I finished the bunker-- when I finished, I went again.
I went by myself.
No, I took [?
Belcher ?] with me always.
I took [?
Belcher ?] with me to go to my Polish friend.
Went there and tell him what I need, that he should build for me from metal an oven, a metal oven, to burn with wood and with pipes that the smoke goes out the pipes.
And I told him to make like two three footers-- actually, two or three footers-- the pipe should be one go on top of the other.
Now the pipe should go higher than the trees, that the-- it wouldn't smoke up the trees.
Once it would smoke up the trees, somebody would spot it, would say something is funny here.
So in the daytime, I took down all the pipes one by one, hide them inside, have a little tree put on top.
And I also told him to make me pipes-- metal pipes-- to make me for air.
I put them in the trees, metal pipes from the bunker, because the bunker was completely closed up, completely, because I had a special thing to close down.
And that's what-- and I told him where to-- I told him where to put it and to bring me some food.
So he brought me.
Now, once more, I went there.
He tell me, Leon, I have to get out.
The Pollacks, the Ukrainians, are doing terrible to us.
They throw sticks with fire.
They want to burn us down.
They chased out in the other of village.
And [INAUDIBLE] they took.
They took the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, the senator.
They took the priest.
They took all the dignitaries from the Polish village.
They put them to a wagon with ropes, and the horses should run fast.
And that's the way they drag them so long till they killed them all.
And that's what I'm afraid they are going to do to us.
So we better have to move on.
I have some food, but I cannot take with me.
You want to come over, you can take and then whatever I have left over, I'll bring you some to the woods.
So we had for a while.
And that's when he disappears.
And I don't know till now where he is.
He moved out someplace in Poland.
Stretching, cut the weeds.
I don't know where I'm looking.
Through the Red Cross, I can't find.
Without his, I wouldn't survive.
He brought me all that stuff, and this put us every day.
It was hard.
It was very hard, because I have to put all the trees while I build the bunker, later put straw I brought from the fields.
Straw put in, wood, straw, branches, and then the trees put in-- putting on top.
Build up the whole thing one by other, there's nobody can come in there.
There now when I build this bunker, I figured sometime it happen, we're going in the fields to dig potatoes.
